rationale: This is the classical textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (sulfuric acid) catalysis. The carbocation is then trapped by acetonitrile, which, after hydrolysis, yields N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and lack complex functional groups, making it a clear and unambiguous representation of the core transformation.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic compounds (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with reactions involving arenes, complex ring systems, or other reactive functional groups. The core transformation is the C-N bond formation between a carbocation and a nitrile, followed by hydrolysis to an amide. This specific and simple topology is unlikely to be confused with other named reactions.
